title: 2 Kings 10
subtitle: The Purge — Jehu Destroys Baal Worship

sections.header: Verses 1–17 — Seventy Sons of Ahab Killed; the House of Ahab Destroyed

refs.note: “I will cut off from Ahab every last male.” Jehu executes this sentence literally.
Hosea will later condemn Jehu’s “massacre at Jezreel” — suggesting that Jehu exceeded his commission.

notes.note: Jehu’s use of prophecy to justify bloodshed warns us that Scripture can be weaponised. The word of God must be handled with reverence, not wielded as a political instrument. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: “Seventy sons” may include grandsons and other male descendants. The number seventy is conventional for a large royal family (cf. Judg 8:30; 9:5). The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

wiseman.source: Donald J. Wiseman, 1 and 2 Kings, Tyndale OT Commentary (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The demand for seventy heads sent in baskets is attested in ANE military practice. Assyrian reliefs from Nineveh show piles of enemy heads at city gates. Jehu’s tactic combines ANE military convention with prophetic justification. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

provan.source: Iain W. Provan, 1 and 2 Kings, New International Biblical Commentary (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Jehu’s “zeal for the LORD” is genuine but also self-serving. The narrator presents it without endorsement, allowing the reader to see both the legitimacy of the prophetic commission and the excess of its execution. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.context: Jehu sends letters to Samaria’s leaders demanding the heads of Ahab’s seventy sons. The elders comply, sending the heads in baskets. Jehu stacks them at the gate of Jezreel — then uses them to make a theological argument: “I conspired against my master and killed him, but who killed all these? Know that not a word the LORD has spoken against the house of Ahab will fail.” He kills everyone connected to Ahab’s house, then encounters forty-two relatives of Ahaziah of Judah and kills them too. The purge is comprehensive and brutal.

heb.paragraph: Bayit (house) carries a deliberate double meaning in Chronicles: Solomon builds God a house (temple), and God promises David a house (dynasty). The interplay between these two 'houses' generates the theological tension that drives the entire narrative: will the dynasty endure? Will the temple remain? Both depend on covenant faithfulness.

notes.note: These genealogical records are not mere ancient census data but the sovereign hand of God tracing His redemptive purposes through specific family lines. Every name preserved here testifies to God's faithfulness in maintaining the covenant lineage that would ultimately produce the Messiah (Matt 1:1-17; Luke 3:23-38).

God's meticulous preservation of these genealogical records demonstrates His commitment to His covenantal promises. Each generation listed is a link in the chain of redemptive history, connecting the patriarchal promises to their ultimate fulfillment in Christ. God does not forget His Word, even across centuries of human failure.

The judgment of exile was not the end of God's purposes but a severe mercy designed to purify and restore. God disciplines those He loves (Heb 12:6). For the believer, seasons of divine discipline are not evidence of God's abandonment but of His fatherly care. He prunes every branch that bears fruit so that it will bear even more (John 15:2).

sections.header: Verses 18–36 — Baal Worshippers Trapped and Killed; Verdict on Jehu

refs.note: Elijah killed 450 prophets of Baal at Carmel. Jehu finishes what Elijah started — but with institutional destruction rather than prophetic contest.
The law commanding total destruction of cities that worship other gods. Jehu executes this against Baal worship but ignores the golden calves.

notes.note: Jehu’s failure teaches that dramatic acts of reform are worthless without sustained personal holiness. It is easier to destroy an enemy’s temple than to purify one’s own heart. External revolution without internal transformation is theatre. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The temple of Baal was converted into a lātrîn (latrine/refuse dump) — the ultimate desecration. The deliberate defilement ensured the site could never be reconsecrated for worship. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

wiseman.source: Donald J. Wiseman, 1 and 2 Kings, Tyndale OT Commentary (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The Black Obelisk of Shalmaneser III shows Jehu (or his ambassador) prostrating before the Assyrian king and paying tribute. This extra-biblical evidence confirms Jehu’s historicity and reveals that his anti-Baal revolution did not protect him from Assyrian domination.

provan.source: Iain W. Provan, 1 and 2 Kings, New International Biblical Commentary (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The Jeroboam clause is the narrator’s permanent standard: every Israelite king is measured against Jeroboam’s foundational sin. No amount of Baal-destroying zeal compensates for maintaining the alternative worship system. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.context: Jehu announces: “Ahab served Baal a little; Jehu will serve him much.” It’s a trap. He summons every Baal priest and worshipper to a great sacrifice, then seals the temple and sends soldiers to kill them all. The Baal temple is demolished and turned into a latrine. Yet the Deuteronomistic verdict is devastating: “Jehu was not careful to keep the law of the LORD with all his heart. He did not turn away from the sins of Jeroboam.” He destroyed Baal but kept the golden calves. Zeal for reform that stops short of full obedience is still failure.

heb.paragraph: The miqdāš (sanctuary) is the place where heaven and earth overlap — where God's presence dwells among His people in a mediated, structured way. The Chronicler devotes extraordinary attention to the sanctuary because it represents the centre of Israel's identity: a people organized around the worship of their God.

notes.note: The record of royal wickedness serves as a sober warning that privilege and position offer no immunity from the consequences of sin. Each king who 'did evil in the eyes of the LORD' demonstrates that departure from God's Word leads inexorably to moral and national decline. The pattern underscores humanity's desperate need for a perfectly righteous King — fulfilled only in Christ.

Idolatry is never merely a theological error — it is spiritual adultery against the covenant God (Jer 3:6-10). The consistent pattern of Kings and Chronicles shows that doctrinal compromise leads inevitably to moral collapse. The church today faces the same danger: any deviation from the exclusive worship of the true God opens the door to every form of evil.

The practical lesson for every believer is that obedience to God's Word is the only path to genuine blessing. The kings who prospered spiritually were those who feared the Lord and walked in His ways. The same principle governs the Christian life: 'If you love Me, keep My commandments' (John 14:15). Obedience is not the means of salvation but the evidence of it.
